backups.title: Nigeria's Next Generation of Founders: Empowering Women and Youth

before.content:
As I stood on the banks of the Ogun River, watching a fish farmer expertly navigate the challenges of scaling her feed supply and cold-chain logistics, I was struck by the enormity of the opportunity before her. With the right support and funding, she could not only grow her business but also create a ripple effect of economic growth throughout her community. However, as I looked closer, I saw the obstacles that lay in her path: limited access to capital, inadequate infrastructure, and a lack of mentorship. These are the same challenges that many women and youth founders in Nigeria face, and it is imperative that we address them head-on.

Understanding the Funding Landscape

The funding landscape for women and youth founders in Nigeria is complex and multifaceted. On one hand, there are numerous opportunities for grants and loans, such as the 6 Best Startup Funding Opportunities for Nigerian Youths in 2026, which can provide much-needed capital for early-stage businesses. For instance, the I&P Acceleration WE4A Program 2026 offers €22,000 in funding for women entrepreneurs, a significant boost for those looking to scale their operations. On the other hand, navigating this landscape can be daunting, especially for those without prior experience. As one founder noted, "I spent months researching funding options, only to realize that I didn't meet the eligibility criteria for most of them."

Practical Application Tactics

So, how can women and youth founders in Nigeria increase their chances of securing funding? First and foremost, it is essential to develop a solid business plan, one that outlines clear goals, revenue projections, and a detailed budget. This will not only help founders stay focused but also demonstrate to potential investors that they have a clear vision for their business. For example, a young entrepreneur in Lagos, who secured funding for her fashion brand, credited her success to a well-crafted business plan that highlighted her unique value proposition. Additionally, founders should be prepared to network and build relationships with potential investors, as these connections can often lead to valuable mentorship and funding opportunities.

Avoiding Scams

As women and youth founders navigate the funding landscape, it is crucial that they are aware of the potential scams and pitfalls that can derail their progress. Red flags include requests for upfront fees, guarantees of funding, and lack of transparency about the funding process. For instance, a founder in Abuja was approached by an "investor" who promised to provide funding in exchange for a significant equity stake, only to discover that the investor was not legitimate. Founders should always do their due diligence, researching the investor or funding organization thoroughly before engaging with them.

Empowering Women and Youth Founders

Empowering women and youth founders in Nigeria requires a multifaceted approach, one that addresses the unique challenges they face. This includes providing access to mentorship, training, and networking opportunities, as well as advocating for policies that support their growth. As one expert noted, "women and youth founders are not just looking for funding, they are looking for a supportive ecosystem that can help them navigate the complexities of building a business." By providing this support, we can unlock the full potential of these founders and create a more inclusive and vibrant entrepreneurial landscape in Nigeria. This is an area where the Nigerian government and private sector can work together to create a supportive business environment that fosters growth and innovation. Furthermore, initiatives such as the Ultimate Guide to Funding for Women Entrepreneurs in Africa can provide valuable resources and guidance for founders looking to scale their businesses.

What to Do This Week

To take the first step towards securing funding and growing your business, consider the following next steps: * Research funding opportunities, such as the 2025 GET Compass Grant Program, which offers grants up to N2 million for MSMEs * Develop a solid business plan, including clear goals, revenue projections, and a detailed budget * Network and build relationships with potential investors, attending events and joining online communities to connect with like-minded founders * Review and understand the terms and conditions of any funding opportunity, ensuring that you are not committing to unrealistic expectations * Seek out mentorship and training programs, such as the I&P Acceleration WE4A Program, to gain valuable insights and skills * Stay up-to-date with the latest developments in the funding landscape, following reputable sources and staying informed about new opportunities and initiatives. By taking these steps, you can increase your chances of success and create a thriving business that contributes to the growth and development of Nigeria's economy.

   Also read: Inflation and how it affects your money.

   Also read: Breaking free from the trap: Loans, Gambling and Financial intelligence.

   Also read: The dangers of Procrastination.

Sources

  • 6 Best Startup Funding Opportunities for Nigerian Youths in 2026 (Grants & Loans) - MSME Africa
  • A Curated List of Grant Opportunities for Nigeria in 2026 - fundsforNGOs

backups.title: Nigeria's Agribusiness Revolution: Funding the Value Chain

before.content:
As I sat with Mrs. Ademola, the owner of a small catering business in Ibadan, she expressed her frustration with the current cash-based system, eager to transition to invoices and formalize her transactions with small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs). However, she was uncertain about the necessary steps to secure funding for her agribusiness, which relied heavily on the agricultural value chain. This challenge is all too common in Nigeria, where agribusinesses face significant hurdles in accessing capital, equipment, and reliable off-take contracts, hindering their growth and potential. The recent affirmation by the CBN governor that the banking sector recapitalization is on course may bring some relief, but it is crucial to explore the specific funding options available for agribusinesses, such as government-facilitated agric loans.

Understanding Agribusiness Funding Options

Nigeria's agribusiness sector can benefit from various funding options, including government initiatives, private sector investments, and international partnerships. For instance, the Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN) has implemented policies to support agricultural development, such as the Anchor Borrowers' Programme, which provides loans to small-scale farmers. Additionally, organizations like the Bank of Industry (BOI) offer financing options for agribusinesses, with a focus on value chain development. As Mrs. Ademola navigated the complex funding landscape, she discovered that the BOI's funding options were more suitable for her business needs.

Equipment and Technology

Access to modern equipment and technology is essential for agribusinesses to increase efficiency and productivity. In Oyo State, for example, the government has established a tractor rental service to support farmers in mechanizing their operations. Similarly, private companies are introducing innovative solutions, such as precision agriculture and irrigation systems, to enhance crop yields and reduce waste. By leveraging these technologies, agribusinesses can improve their competitiveness and attract investors. A visit to the International Institute of Tropical Agriculture (IITA) in Ibadan revealed the potential of technology in transforming the agricultural sector, with experts showcasing drones and satellite imaging for crop monitoring.

Off-take Contracts and Market Access

Secure off-take contracts are critical for agribusinesses to ensure a stable market for their products. In Nigeria, companies like Olam and Dangote have established partnerships with farmers to purchase their produce, providing a guaranteed market and stable prices. Furthermore, initiatives like the African Continental Free Trade Area (AfCFTA) aim to increase market access for Nigerian agribusinesses, expanding their reach beyond local borders. As a small-scale farmer in Kano State, Alhaji Abdullahi, noted, "Having a reliable off-take contract with a reputable company has allowed me to focus on improving my yields and quality, knowing that I have a guaranteed buyer."

Avoiding Scams

When seeking funding or partnerships, it is essential for agribusinesses to be cautious of scams and fraudulent activities. Red flags include unsolicited investment offers, unusually high returns, and requests for upfront payments. Agribusinesses should research potential partners thoroughly, verify their credentials, and seek advice from reputable organizations, such as the Nigerian Agricultural Insurance Corporation (NAIC). A recent incident in Lagos involved a fake investment scheme, where unsuspecting farmers lost millions of naira to scammers promising unusually high returns on their investments.

What to Do This Week

To take advantage of the agribusiness funding opportunities in Nigeria, consider the following steps: * Research government initiatives, such as the CBN's Anchor Borrowers' Programme, and explore private sector funding options, like the BOI's financing schemes. * Investigate equipment and technology providers, such as tractor rental services and precision agriculture companies. * Develop a business plan and financial projections to attract investors and secure off-take contracts. * Network with other agribusinesses and industry experts to learn about best practices and potential partnerships. * Register your business with relevant organizations, such as the NAIC, to access support services and insurance options. * Explore trade infrastructure financing options, such as those offered by the Africa Policy Research Institute, to improve your business's competitiveness. For more information on agribusiness funding and growth, consider exploring topics like Nigeria's agribusiness value chain, trade infrastructure financing in Africa, and small business grants.

   Also read: Inflation and how it affects your money.

   Also read: Breaking free from the trap: Loans, Gambling and Financial intelligence.

   Also read: The dangers of Procrastination.

Sources

  • CBN governor affirms banking sector recapitalisation is on course - Financial Nigeria
  • Recovering government-facilitated agric loans - The Guardian Nigeria News

backups.title: Nigeria's Tech Operators: Navigating B2B SaaS and Funding Challenges

before.content:
As she navigated the cramped aisles of her pharmacy in Enugu, Mrs. Okoro struggled to keep track of her inventory and compliance costs, the weight of restocking cycles and regulatory requirements threatening to overwhelm her small business. This is a common challenge faced by many entrepreneurs in Nigeria, where the lack of efficient systems and limited access to funding can hinder growth and profitability. For B2B SaaS operators, the problem is even more pronounced, as they must balance product traction with the need to secure funding in a tight market. The recent announcement of the 6 Best Startup Funding Opportunities for Nigerian Youths in 2026 is a welcome development, but it also highlights the need for a more nuanced understanding of the funding landscape.

Understanding the Funding Landscape

The Nigerian funding landscape is characterized by a mix of grants, loans, and investments, with a growing focus on supporting women-led startups and agribusinesses. For instance, the AgroDevCo Agribusiness Financing Opportunity 2026 is a notable example of the efforts being made to support the agricultural sector. However, for B2B SaaS operators, the key challenge is to identify the right funding opportunities and to develop a robust growth strategy that can withstand the pressures of a tight funding market. As one founder noted, "we had to pivot our business model to focus on enterprise sales, which has helped us to build a more stable revenue stream."

Building Product Traction

Building product traction is critical for B2B SaaS operators, as it provides a foundation for growth and funding. This involves developing a deep understanding of the target market and creating a product that meets the needs of customers. For example, a startup that provides inventory management solutions to pharmacies like Mrs. Okoro's can help to reduce compliance costs and improve efficiency. By focusing on product traction, B2B SaaS operators can build a loyal customer base and create a robust revenue stream.

Avoiding Scams

In the pursuit of funding, it is essential to avoid scams and fraudulent activities. Red flags include unsolicited emails or messages promising guaranteed funding, requests for upfront fees, and lack of transparency about the funding process. As one entrepreneur warned, "we were approached by a 'funding provider' who promised us a large sum of money, but it turned out to be a scam." It is crucial to do thorough research and due diligence before engaging with any funding opportunity.

Managing Runway

Managing runway is critical for B2B SaaS operators, as it determines how long the business can survive without funding. This involves developing a robust financial plan, reducing costs, and building a stable revenue stream. For instance, a startup can reduce its burn rate by outsourcing non-core functions and focusing on high-margin products. By managing runway effectively, B2B SaaS operators can create a buffer against funding uncertainty and build a more sustainable business.

Growth Strategies

Growth strategies are essential for B2B SaaS operators, as they provide a roadmap for expansion and funding. This involves identifying new markets, developing strategic partnerships, and building a strong sales team. For example, a startup can partner with a large enterprise to offer its product as a white-label solution, which can help to build credibility and drive revenue growth. By focusing on growth strategies, B2B SaaS operators can build a scalable business that can attract funding and drive profitability.

What to Do This Week

To navigate the challenges of B2B SaaS and funding, entrepreneurs can take the following steps: * Review your financial plan and reduce costs where possible * Develop a robust growth strategy that focuses on product traction and customer acquisition * Research funding opportunities and avoid scams * Build a strong sales team and develop strategic partnerships * Focus on building a stable revenue stream and managing runway * Network with other entrepreneurs and funding providers to build relationships and stay informed about the latest developments in the funding landscape, including the role of startup funding opportunities and agribusiness financing in driving growth.

   Also read: How to succeed as a starter.

   Also read: Financial Growth and management .

   Also read: Government grants.

Sources

  • 6 Best Startup Funding Opportunities for Nigerian Youths in 2026 (Grants & Loans) - MSME Africa
  • Women-led startups to get more loans, investment in capital inflow - Business News Nigeria

backups.title: Nigeria's Funding Landscape: Navigating Fintech and Credit Products

before.content:
It's a typical Monday morning in Kaduna, and Abdullahi, a small solar installer, is on his way to meet a potential client. He has been chasing this large contract for months, and he's hopeful that today will be the day he finally closes the deal. As he arrives at the meeting, he's reminded that the payment terms are 90 days, which will put a strain on his cash flow. This is a common problem faced by many small business owners in Nigeria, who struggle to access funding and manage cash flow due to delayed payments.

Understanding Nigerian Regulation

The Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN) has been working to improve access to funding for small businesses, with initiatives such as capping banking charges to reduce costs for MSMEs and digital transactions. However, many small business owners are still unaware of the options available to them. For instance, Abdullahi could explore merchant lending options, which can provide him with the necessary funding to take on larger contracts. As he considers his options, he meets with a financial advisor who suggests he look into licensed loan apps in Nigeria, which can provide quick and easy access to funding.

Fintech and Credit Products

Fintech companies are playing a crucial role in providing credit products to small businesses in Nigeria. With the rise of digital lending platforms, small business owners can now access funding quickly and easily. However, it's essential to do your research and choose a reputable lender. For example, Abdullahi's friend, Fatima, recently used a licensed loan app to access funding for her fashion business, and she was able to repay the loan within a few months. As Abdullahi considers his options, he's aware that he needs to be careful and avoid scams.

Avoiding Scams

When exploring funding options, it's essential to be aware of the red flags that can indicate a scam. If a lender is asking for upfront fees or guaranteeing approval regardless of credit history, it's likely a scam. Additionally, be wary of lenders who are not transparent about their terms and conditions. Abdullahi's financial advisor warns him to be cautious of lenders who are not registered with the CBN or the Bank of Industry (BOI). As he navigates the funding landscape, he's aware that he needs to do his research and choose a reputable lender.

Microfinance Banks and Merchant Lending

Microfinance banks and merchant lending platforms can provide small business owners with the necessary funding to grow their businesses. For instance, Abdullahi could explore microfinance banks in Nigeria, such as those listed in the top 10 microfinance banks in Nigeria. He could also consider merchant lending options, which can provide him with funding based on his business's cash flow. As he considers his options, he meets with a representative from a microfinance bank who explains the benefits of their loan products.

What to Do This Week

To navigate the funding landscape in Nigeria, small business owners can take the following steps: * Research licensed loan apps in Nigeria and compare their terms and conditions * Explore microfinance banks and their loan products * Consider merchant lending options and how they can provide funding based on cash flow * Be aware of the red flags that can indicate a scam and choose a reputable lender * Meet with a financial advisor to discuss the best funding options for your business * Review your business's cash flow and create a plan to manage delayed payments * Look into initiatives such as the CBN's capping of banking charges to reduce costs for MSMEs and digital transactions, and how they can benefit your business. By taking these steps, small business owners like Abdullahi can access the funding they need to grow their businesses and navigate the challenges of delayed payments. As small business owners navigate the funding landscape, they can explore topics such as credit products for small businesses, fintech and digital lending, and merchant lending options to find the best solutions for their businesses.

   Also read: Financial Growth and management .

   Also read: Government grants.

   Also read: Debt and the Dreamer.

Sources

  • Top Microfinance Banks in Nigeria: 10 Best Picks for 2026 - West Africa Trade Hub
  • Licensed Loan Apps in Nigeria 2025 - Proshare

backups.title: Clean Energy and Climate Programs: A New Frontier for Nigerian Businesses

before.content:
As I walked into the rice mill in Kano, I was struck by the cacophony of sounds and the smell of freshly cut rice. The operator, Abdul, was busy balancing equipment repairs and working capital, a constant juggling act that many small business owners in Nigeria can relate to. But what caught my attention was the diesel generator humming in the background, a constant reminder of the high energy costs that eat into Abdul's already thin profit margins. This scenario is all too common in Nigeria, where access to reliable and affordable energy is a major bottleneck to growth. As the country transitions to a more sustainable energy mix, clean energy and climate programs are emerging as a new frontier for Nigerian businesses.

Solar Energy: A Bright Spot for Nigerian Businesses

The Nigerian government has set ambitious targets to increase the share of renewable energy in the national energy mix, and solar energy is poised to play a major role. With the cost of solar panels decreasing dramatically over the years, many businesses are now turning to solar as a reliable and cost-effective source of energy. For example, a small business in Lagos can now power its operations with a solar-powered mini-grid, reducing its reliance on diesel generators and lowering its energy costs. This not only helps the business bottom line but also contributes to a cleaner environment. Also read: Debt and the Dreamer.

Impact Capital: Funding the Clean Energy Transition

Impact capital is playing a critical role in funding the clean energy transition in Nigeria. Investors are looking for opportunities to support businesses that are working to reduce carbon emissions and promote sustainable development. The African Development Bank's Sustainable Energy Fund for Africa, for instance, has approved $5.65 million to pioneer a new climate finance instrument for off-grid renewable energy projects in Africa's fragile states. This type of funding is crucial for supporting businesses like Abdul's rice mill, which can now access affordable and reliable energy to power its operations.

Avoiding Scams

As the clean energy sector grows, so do the scams. It's essential for businesses to be vigilant when dealing with funding opportunities or partnerships. Red flags include unsolicited emails or phone calls promising unrealistic returns on investment, or requests for upfront fees to access funding or partnerships. For instance, a business owner in Port Harcourt was recently approached by a "funding agency" that promised to provide a large grant to support the development of a solar-powered mini-grid, but only if the business owner paid a "processing fee" upfront. This is a classic scam, and businesses should always do their due diligence and research any potential funding opportunities or partnerships thoroughly. Also read: The investor's daughter.

Micro-Grids and Energy Access

Micro-grids are also emerging as a critical component of the clean energy landscape in Nigeria. These small, localized energy systems can provide reliable and affordable energy to communities and businesses, especially in areas where the national grid is weak or non-existent. For example, a micro-grid project in a rural community in Ogun state can provide energy to households and small businesses, promoting economic development and improving living standards. As the sector grows, we can expect to see more innovative solutions like pay-as-you-go solar home systems, which are already being deployed in many parts of the country. Also read: The loan that built a Legacy.

What to Do This Week

If you're a business owner or entrepreneur looking to tap into the clean energy sector, here are some concrete next steps you can take this week: * Research funding opportunities from organizations like the African Development Bank or the Nigerian government's Rural Electrification Agency * Explore partnerships with companies that specialize in solar energy or micro-grids * Conduct an energy audit of your business to identify areas where you can reduce energy costs and improve efficiency * Look into training programs or workshops that can help you build your skills and knowledge in the clean energy sector * Join online communities or forums where you can connect with other businesses and entrepreneurs who are working in the clean energy space * Review your business plan and identify areas where you can incorporate clean energy solutions to reduce your environmental impact and improve your bottom line. You can also learn more about clean energy financing options, renewable energy policy, and sustainable development initiatives to support your business goals.

Sources

  • Scaling Green Technologies and Youth Employment in African Tech Startups: the Nigerian Solar Mini-Grid Sector - APRI – Africa Policy Research Institute
  • REA, Partners Launch $188m Fund to Develop 191MW Solar Capacity - MSME Africa
  • Multinational: African Development Bank’s Sustainable Energy Fund for Africa Approves $5.65 million to Pioneer New Climate Finance Instrument for Off-Grid Renewable Energy projects in Africa’s Fragile States - African Development Bank Group

backups.title: Nigeria's Creative Economy: Unlocking Growth Through Effective Monetization

before.content:
Nigerian founders in the creative industry face a daunting challenge: transforming their innovative ideas into sustainable businesses. Despite the country's rich cultural heritage and thriving arts scene, many creatives struggle to monetize their work, protect their intellectual property, and access distribution channels. This gap hinders the growth of the creative economy, which has the potential to contribute significantly to Nigeria's GDP. To bridge this gap, it is essential to explore strategies for effective monetization, intellectual property protection, and strategic partnerships that can help Nigerian creatives thrive.

Monetization Strategies for Nigerian Creatives

Nigerian creatives can explore various monetization strategies, including project-based grants, such as the UK-Nigeria Creative Fund 2026, which offers grants for film, fashion, and music projects. Additionally, initiatives like the Creative Economy Development Fund provide financial support to creatives and entrepreneurs in the industry. However, to maximize these opportunities, founders must develop a deep understanding of their target audience, create valuable content, and leverage digital platforms to reach a broader market. Also read: Government grants.

Protecting Intellectual Property in Nigeria's Creative Economy

Intellectual property protection is crucial for Nigerian creatives, as it enables them to safeguard their work and prevent unauthorized use. The Nigerian Copyright Act provides a framework for protecting creative works, but enforcement remains a challenge. To mitigate this risk, founders can register their work with the Nigerian Copyright Commission and use copyright notices to deter infringement. Moreover, collaborating with industry partners and organizations that prioritize IP protection can help creatives navigate the complex landscape.

Distribution and Brand Partnerships: Key to Scaling Creative Businesses

Effective distribution and brand partnerships are essential for scaling creative businesses in Nigeria. By partnering with brands and organizations that share their vision, creatives can access new audiences, gain credibility, and increase their revenue streams. For instance, the Bank of Industry's Creative Industry Loan 2026 provides financing options for creatives to develop and execute their projects. Furthermore, initiatives like the CEDF's Phase 2 Loans offer opportunities for Nigerian creatives and entrepreneurs to access funding and expertise to grow their businesses. Also read: Debt and the Dreamer.

Avoiding Scams in Nigeria's Creative Economy

As the creative economy grows, so do the risks of scams and fraudulent activities. Nigerian creatives must be cautious when dealing with unfamiliar organizations or individuals offering funding, distribution, or partnership opportunities. Red flags to watch out for include: unsolicited emails or messages with promises of guaranteed funding, requests for upfront fees or payments, and lack of transparency about the organization or individual's credentials. To avoid scams, founders should conduct thorough research, verify the credibility of potential partners, and seek advice from trusted industry experts. Also read: The investor's daughter.

What to Do This Week

To take advantage of the opportunities in Nigeria's creative economy, consider the following next steps:

  • Research and apply for project-based grants, such as the UK-Nigeria Creative Fund 2026, to support your creative projects.
  • Register your work with the Nigerian Copyright Commission to protect your intellectual property.
  • Explore distribution channels and brand partnerships that align with your vision and target audience.
  • Develop a comprehensive business plan, including revenue streams, marketing strategies, and financial projections.
  • Network with industry professionals, attend workshops, and join online communities to stay informed about opportunities and best practices in the creative economy.
  • Review and understand the terms and conditions of funding opportunities, such as the BOI Creative Industry Loan 2026, to ensure they align with your business goals.
  • Seek guidance from trusted advisors, such as lawyers, accountants, or industry experts, to navigate the complex landscape of Nigeria's creative economy.

Sources

  • Call for Applications: UK–Nigeria Creative Fund 2026 (Project-Based Grants for Film, Fashion & Music) - MSME Africa
  • Accelerating Nigeria’s Creative Economy: Why the Future May Begin With the Written Word - The Guardian Nigeria News
  • Nigeria’s creative economy needs more than stars. It needs a writing pipeline - Business News Nigeria

backups.title: Unlocking Growth: Empowering Women and Youth Founders in Nigeria

before.content:
Nigerian founders, particularly women and youth, face significant challenges in accessing funding and growth opportunities. Despite the country's vast entrepreneurial potential, many startups struggle to secure investment, scaling their businesses and creating jobs. The lack of access to capital, coupled with limited networking opportunities and mentorship, hinders the growth of these startups, ultimately affecting the country's economic development. However, recent initiatives and programs have emerged to address these challenges, providing a glimmer of hope for women and youth founders in Nigeria.

Women-Led Startups on the Rise

Women-led startups in Nigeria are gaining traction, with several initiatives and programs aimed at supporting their growth. The FCMB MFB Business Acceleration Program, for instance, has opened its doors to applications, offering a platform for women entrepreneurs to access funding, mentorship, and networking opportunities. Similarly, the MTN Foundation Y'ellopreneur Program 2026 is dedicated to empowering Nigerian female entrepreneurs, providing them with the necessary tools and resources to succeed. These programs demonstrate a growing recognition of the importance of supporting women-led startups in Nigeria. Also read: The dangers of Procrastination.

Youth Founders: Tapping into Nigeria's Demographic Dividend

Nigeria's youth population presents a significant opportunity for economic growth and development. With over 60% of the population under the age of 25, the country has a vast pool of talented and ambitious young entrepreneurs. Programs such as the 2025 GET Compass Grant Program, which offers grants up to ₦2 million for MSMEs, are helping to tap into this demographic dividend. By providing youth founders with access to funding, mentorship, and training, these programs are empowering them to turn their ideas into successful businesses, driving innovation and job creation in the process. Also read: How to succeed as a starter.

Funding Opportunities for Women and Youth Founders

Several funding opportunities are available for women and youth founders in Nigeria. The Ultimate Guide to Funding for Women Entrepreneurs in Africa, for example, provides a comprehensive overview of the various funding options available, including grants, loans, and investments. Additionally, the March 2026 deadlines for funding opportunities, as outlined by fundsforNGOs, highlight the importance of staying informed about upcoming deadlines and applying for relevant funding programs. By accessing these funding opportunities, women and youth founders can secure the necessary capital to scale their businesses and achieve their growth objectives. Also read: Financial Growth and management .

Avoiding Scams

When exploring funding opportunities, it is essential for women and youth founders to be cautious of scams. Red flags to watch out for include: unsolicited emails or messages offering funding or investment opportunities, requests for upfront fees or payments, and lack of transparency about the funding process or requirements. Founders should also be wary of programs that guarantee funding or investment, as these are often scams. By being vigilant and doing their due diligence, founders can avoid falling prey to these scams and focus on securing legitimate funding opportunities.

What to Do This Week

  • Research and review the FCMB MFB Business Acceleration Program and the MTN Foundation Y'ellopreneur Program 2026 to determine if your startup is eligible for these initiatives.
  • Review the Ultimate Guide to Funding for Women Entrepreneurs in Africa to explore the various funding options available and identify potential opportunities for your business.
  • Check the fundsforNGOs website for upcoming deadlines for funding opportunities and apply for relevant programs.
  • Network with other women and youth founders to share experiences, best practices, and advice on accessing funding and growing your business.
  • Develop a comprehensive business plan, including a detailed financial projection, to prepare your startup for funding applications and investment opportunities.
  • Stay informed about the latest developments in Nigeria's startup ecosystem, including new funding initiatives, programs, and policies that can support your business growth.

Sources

  • Women-led startups to get more loans, investment in capital inflow - Business News Nigeria
  • Call for Applications: FCMB MFB Business Acceleration Program Opens - MSME Africa
  • March 2026 Deadlines: Funding You Shouldn’t Miss - fundsforNGOs

backups.title: Nigeria's Agribusiness Funding Conundrum: Unlocking Growth in the Value Chain

before.content:
Nigerian founders in the agribusiness sector face a daunting challenge: securing funding to scale their operations and bring their products to market. With the majority of Nigeria's population engaged in agriculture, the potential for growth is vast, yet many entrepreneurs struggle to access the necessary capital, equipment, and off-take contracts to make their businesses viable. According to a recent report, corruption and inefficiencies in government assistance programs have negated support for small businesses, leaving many to fend for themselves. The situation is further complicated by the lack of clear policies and infrastructure to support the agribusiness value chain, making it difficult for founders to navigate the complex landscape.

Understanding the Agribusiness Value Chain

The agribusiness value chain encompasses a range of activities, from input supply and production to processing, marketing, and distribution. In Nigeria, this chain is often fragmented, with many small-scale farmers and processors struggling to access markets and secure fair prices for their products. To address this, the government has initiated various programs, such as the Anchor Borrowers' Program, aimed at providing financing and support to small-scale farmers. However, the impact of these initiatives has been limited, and more needs to be done to create an enabling environment for agribusinesses to thrive. Also read: Inflation and how it affects your money.

Accessing Funding and Equipment

Securing funding and equipment is a significant hurdle for many Nigerian agribusinesses. While there are various funding options available, such as the Central Bank of Nigeria's (CBN) Agricultural Credit Guarantee Scheme, the process of accessing these funds can be cumbersome and time-consuming. Additionally, the cost of equipment, such as tractors and irrigation systems, can be prohibitively expensive for many small-scale farmers. To address this, some organizations, such as the Bank of Industry (BOI), offer financing options and leasing arrangements for equipment, which can help reduce the upfront costs for farmers.

Off-take Contracts and Market Access

Off-take contracts, which guarantee a market for agricultural products, are essential for agribusinesses to secure a stable income stream. However, many small-scale farmers in Nigeria struggle to access these contracts, as they often lack the necessary scale and infrastructure to meet the demands of large buyers. To address this, some companies, such as those involved in the Anchor Borrowers' Program, offer off-take contracts to small-scale farmers, providing them with a guaranteed market for their products. Furthermore, initiatives such as the Nigerian Agricultural Insurance Corporation can help mitigate the risks associated with farming, making it more attractive for farmers to invest in their operations. Also read: Breaking free from the trap: Loans, Gambling and Financial intelligence.

Avoiding Scams

When seeking funding and support for their agribusinesses, Nigerian founders need to be cautious of scams and unscrupulous operators. Red flags to watch out for include: unsolicited emails or messages offering funding or equipment at unrealistically low rates, requests for upfront payments or fees, and companies or individuals with no verifiable track record or physical address. It is essential to conduct thorough research and due diligence on any potential partners or funders, and to seek advice from reputable sources, such as the CBN or BOI, before committing to any agreements. Also read: The dangers of Procrastination.

What to Do This Week

To take the first steps towards unlocking growth in the agribusiness value chain, consider the following actions:

  • Research and identify potential funding options, such as the CBN's Agricultural Credit Guarantee Scheme or the BOI's financing programs
  • Reach out to organizations, such as the Nigerian Agricultural Insurance Corporation, to learn more about their services and how they can support your agribusiness
  • Attend industry events and conferences to network with other entrepreneurs and potential partners
  • Develop a comprehensive business plan, including a clear outline of your production costs, marketing strategy, and financial projections
  • Visit the websites of government agencies, such as the CBN and BOI, to stay up-to-date on the latest initiatives and programs supporting the agribusiness sector
  • Join online forums and groups, such as those on social media, to connect with other agribusiness founders and stay informed about industry developments

Sources

  • Enhancing Nigeria’s Clean Energy Transition: Mapping Chinese Investment and Strategic Priorities - APRI – Africa Policy Research Institute
  • Small Business Grants: 40+ Free Funding Options (2026) - Shopify
  • The Buhari administration at 6: Counting the Blessings One By One By Femi Adesina - - TVC News

backups.title: Nigeria Funding and Opportunities Briefing: part 3

before.content:
Nigeria is abuzz with fresh funding opportunities and policy updates that can propel businesses and projects forward. Across various sectors, including technology, healthcare, agriculture, education, manufacturing, clean energy, and the creative economy, there are numerous grants, loans, accelerators, and market programs available. In this briefing, we will explore some of these opportunities and provide practical moves for individuals and businesses to capitalize on them.

Sectoral Opportunities

In the agriculture sector, the Comprehensive List of Funding Opportunities for Agriculture and Farming by fundsforNGOs is a valuable resource. It outlines various grants and loans that farmers and agricultural businesses can access to improve their operations and increase productivity. Similarly, the September 2025 Funding Opportunities by Substack highlights 19 new opportunities in agriculture, climate, environment, energy, and food. These opportunities are crucial for Nigeria's economic growth, given the significant contribution of agriculture to the country's GDP. Also read: The investor's daughter.

In the clean energy sector, the AfCFTA and Green Job Opportunities in Africa by APRI underscore the importance of sustainable progress and green job creation. With Nigeria's commitment to reducing carbon emissions and increasing the use of renewable energy, there are emerging opportunities for businesses and innovators in this sector. The 22 Real Grant Opportunities Many Founders Ignore (But Shouldn’t) by naijapreneur also lists several grants that can be accessed by startups and small businesses in the clean energy and environmental sectors.

Practical Moves

To take advantage of these opportunities, here are three practical moves readers can take today: Also read: The loan that built a Legacy.

  • Research and Identify Relevant Opportunities: Spend time researching the various funding opportunities available in your sector. Look for grants, loans, and accelerators that align with your business goals and objectives.
  • Review and Meet Eligibility Criteria: Once you have identified relevant opportunities, review the eligibility criteria carefully. Ensure that you meet all the requirements before applying to avoid rejection.
  • Prepare a Strong Application: If you meet the eligibility criteria, prepare a strong application. This includes developing a solid business plan, crafting a compelling proposal, and gathering all necessary documents.

Additionally, consider reaching out to organizations that offer funding opportunities to inquire about their application processes and requirements. This can help you stay ahead of the curve and increase your chances of securing funding.

Avoiding Scams

While there are numerous legitimate funding opportunities available, there are also scams that individuals and businesses should be aware of. To avoid scams, never pay a fee to apply for a grant or loan, and be cautious of opportunities that seem too good to be true. Always research the organization offering the funding opportunity and verify their legitimacy before applying. Also read: Inflation and how it affects your money.

Next Steps

With these opportunities and practical moves in mind, the next step is to take action. Start by researching funding opportunities in your sector and reviewing the eligibility criteria. Prepare a strong application, and reach out to organizations that offer funding opportunities to inquire about their application processes and requirements. By taking these steps, you can increase your chances of securing funding and propel your business or project forward. The Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN) and the Bank of Industry (BOI) also offer various funding opportunities and loans to businesses in Nigeria, so be sure to explore these options as well. With the right funding and support, Nigerian businesses and innovators can drive growth, create jobs, and contribute to the country's economic development.

Sources

  • March 2026 Deadlines: Funding You Shouldn’t Miss - fundsforNGOs
  • Fidelity Bank Begins Disbursement Of N5bn Credit Guarantee Facility - The Whistler Newspaper
  • Sustainable Progress: The AfCFTA and Green Job Opportunities in Africa | APRI - APRI – Africa Policy Research Institute

As we continue to navigate the Nigerian business landscape, it's essential to stay informed about the latest funding opportunities and policy updates. In this briefing, we'll explore a mix of sectors where funding is needed, including technology, healthcare, agriculture, education, manufacturing, clean energy, and the creative economy.

Current Opportunities

Nigerian entrepreneurs and businesses can take advantage of various grants, loans, accelerators, and market programs. For instance, the ₦5bn credit guarantee facility disbursed by Fidelity Bank is a great opportunity for small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs) to access funding. Additionally, the AfCFTA and green job opportunities in Africa can provide a platform for businesses to expand their reach and create sustainable jobs.

In the agriculture sector, the Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN) and the Bank of Industry (BOI) have implemented policies to support farmers and agricultural businesses. The CBN's Anchor Borrowers' Programme, for example, provides loans to farmers at a low interest rate, while the BOI's Agricultural Fund provides financing for agricultural projects. Also read: The investor's daughter.

In the technology sector, startups can apply for grants and funding from organizations such as the National Information Technology Development Agency (NITDA) and the Nigerian Communications Commission (NCC). These organizations provide funding for innovative projects and startups that aim to solve real-world problems.

Practical Moves for Today

Here are three to five practical moves you can take today to access funding and stay updated on opportunities: Also read: The loan that built a Legacy.

  • Visit the Fidelity Bank website to learn more about the ₦5bn credit guarantee facility and how to apply.
  • Check the CBN and BOI websites for updates on agricultural funding and policies.
  • Explore the NITDA and NCC websites for technology-related grants and funding opportunities.
  • Register for the SME Grants program, which is closing in March 2026, to access funding for your small business.
  • Research the AfCFTA and green job opportunities in Africa to learn more about expanding your business and creating sustainable jobs.

Avoiding Scams

When searching for funding opportunities, it's essential to be cautious of scams. Here are some tips to avoid scams:

  • Only apply for funding through official websites and channels.
  • Never pay a fee to apply for funding or to access a grant.
  • Research the organization or company offering the funding to ensure it's legitimate.
  • Be wary of opportunities that seem too good to be true or require you to provide sensitive information.

Next Steps

To stay updated on the latest funding opportunities and policy updates, we recommend: Also read: Inflation and how it affects your money.

  • Following reputable news sources and websites, such as Businessamlive and The Whistler Newspaper.
  • Registering for newsletters and updates from organizations such as the CBN, BOI, NITDA, and NCC.
  • Networking with other entrepreneurs and businesses to share information and resources.
  • Attending workshops and seminars to learn more about funding opportunities and policy updates.

By taking these practical moves and staying informed, you can access the funding and resources you need to grow your business and succeed in the Nigerian market.

Sources

  • March 2026 Deadlines: Funding You Shouldn’t Miss - fundsforNGOs
  • Fidelity Bank Begins Disbursement Of N5bn Credit Guarantee Facility - The Whistler Newspaper
  • Building Nigeria’s bridges with smart financing, smart spending - Businessamlive

backups.title: Nigeria Funding & Grants Briefing for Entrepreneurs

before.content:
As we approach the end of the first quarter of 2026, the Nigerian entrepreneurial landscape is witnessing a surge in grant and loan opportunities. With the Small and Medium Enterprises Development Agency of Nigeria (SMEDAN) planning to formalize one million businesses and provide a ₦12 billion funding boost, the time to act is now. In this briefing, we will outline the latest developments and provide actionable steps for founders to secure funding.

Practical Moves for Founders

To take advantage of the current funding landscape, entrepreneurs can take the following steps:

  • Review the list of business grants available in Nigeria and submit applications before the March 2026 deadline. This includes opportunities from SMEDAN and other government agencies.
  • Explore the complete guide to getting a business loan in Nigeria, which outlines the requirements and procedures for securing funding from various lenders.
  • Prepare a solid business plan and financial projections to increase the chances of securing grants or loans. This includes developing a clear understanding of the business model, market, and financial requirements.
  • Register and formalize the business to access funding opportunities and benefits from government agencies such as SMEDAN.

Avoiding Scams

While there are legitimate funding opportunities available, it is essential to be cautious of scams and fraudulent schemes. To avoid falling victim, entrepreneurs should: Also read: How to succeed as a starter.

Never pay a fee to secure a grant or loan, as this is often a sign of a scam. Legitimate funding opportunities do not require upfront payments.

Verify the authenticity of funding opportunities by researching the organization and contacting them directly to confirm the details.

Be wary of unsolicited emails or messages offering funding opportunities, as these are often phishing scams. Also read: Financial Growth and management .

Next Steps

To stay updated on the latest funding opportunities and policy updates, entrepreneurs can:

Visit the SMEDAN website and social media channels to stay informed about the latest developments and funding opportunities.

Follow reputable business and financial news sources to stay up-to-date on the latest trends and updates in the Nigerian entrepreneurial landscape. Also read: Government grants.

Contact the Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN) and the Bank of Industry (BOI) to inquire about available funding opportunities and requirements.

By taking these practical steps and staying informed, entrepreneurs can increase their chances of securing funding and growing their businesses in Nigeria.

Sources

  • Business grants available in Nigeria in 2026 — and how to apply - Tribune Online
  • Business Loan in Nigeria: How to Get Funding in 2026 (Complete Guide) - nigeriahousingmarket.com
  • SMEDAN plans one million business formalisation, N12bn funding boost in 2026 - Punch Newspapers
